title: Woven cushion cover on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: A plain-weave cushion cover in two matching panels, woven on a rigid heddle loom and sewn together, approximately 45 × 45 cm
excerpt: A cushion cover is a practical first finished project on a rigid heddle loom: the size is manageable on a 60 cm loom, the plain weave suits beginners, and the finished object is used rather than displayed. This entry covers warping, weaving two matching panels, and assembling the cover with a zip closure.
sourceNotes: Rigid heddle cushion cover pattern synthesised from general rigid heddle weaving practice. The two-panel construction is a standard approach for cushion covers woven on looms narrower than the desired finished width.

glossaryTerms.definition: The number of warp ends per centimetre, controlled by the slot and hole spacing in the rigid heddle. For a cushion cover in DK weight wool, a sett of 6-7 ends per cm gives a balanced plain weave.
The set of threads running lengthways through the rigid heddle and tensioned on the loom. The warp provides the structure of the woven cloth.
The thread carried across the warp on each pick. For a cushion cover, the weft colour typically matches or complements the warp.
A plain weave in which the warp and weft are equally visible: the sett and weft packing are matched so neither dominates. Balanced weave produces a square, even cloth.

content.text: A cushion cover is woven in two panels of identical size, then sewn together with the right sides facing. The rigid heddle loom makes this practical because both panels are warped and woven from the same chain, keeping the

in a medium-weight wool at 6-7 ends per cm produces cloth with good body for a cushion without being stiff. Allow for 15-20% shrinkage in both directions when calculating the warp length and weaving width.

items.name: Warp yarn, wool DK weight, main colour
Weft yarn, matching or coordinating colour
Rigid heddle loom, 60 cm weaving width

content.text: Step 1: Calculate and wind the warp
For a finished cushion cover of 45 × 45 cm, allow for shrinkage: wind the

to 52 cm wide in the heddle and 110 cm long per panel (two panels plus loom waste). Total warp length: approximately 250 cm. At 6 ends per cm, the warp width of 52 cm requires 312 ends. Wind onto the warping board or pegs at a consistent tension, maintaining a threading cross.

Thread alternately: one end through a slot, the next through a hole, across the full width of the heddle. This gives the standard rigid heddle plain weave shed. Beam the warp onto the back beam under firm, even tension. Tie the front ends onto the front beam in small bundles, adjusting tension until the warp surface is level and even.

Weave the first panel, counting picks carefully. Record the number of picks woven per centimetre and maintain the same rate throughout both panels. Mark the end of the first panel with a contrasting pick of waste yarn, then continue weaving the second panel to the same pick count. Consistent weft packing is critical: if the second panel has a different weft density it will not match the first when sewn together.
Step 4: Finish the cloth and sew the cover

Cut the cloth from the loom. Wash by hand in warm water and press flat while damp to set the weave and achieve the final dimensions. Trim to 45 × 45 cm panels plus 1.5 cm seam allowance on all sides. Sew the zip into one edge of each panel before joining. Sew the remaining three sides with right sides together, press the seams open, and turn right side out. Insert the cushion pad.

items.problem: The two panels are different lengths after washing
items.cause: The weft packing differed between panels
items.fix: Work from a pick count rather than a centimetre measurement while weaving. Count every 10 picks and check the height with a ruler to maintain a consistent rate.

items.problem: The cloth draws in significantly at the selvedges and is much narrower than the heddle width
items.cause: The weft was pulled too tight before beating
items.fix: Let the weft arc across the shed before beating. Push the arc down gently with a finger before lowering the heddle to beat.

items.problem: The woven cloth is stiffer than expected after washing
items.cause: The sett is too close for the yarn weight, producing a warp-faced rather than balanced cloth
items.fix: For future projects, use a heddle with a larger slot spacing. A warp-faced cushion cover is still functional but will be stiffer and heavier than a balanced weave.
